cards.definition: nutrients needed in large amounts — carbohydrates, proteins, and fats (lipids)
the main source of energy — simple (sugars) or complex (starch). Found in bread, rice, pasta, fruit, and sugar
needed for growth, repair, and maintenance of the body — made of amino acids. Found in meat, fish, eggs, and pulses
amino acids the body cannot make — must be obtained from food. Found in complete proteins like meat and eggs
protein containing all essential amino acids — found in animal sources: meat, fish, eggs, dairy
protein lacking one or more essential amino acids — found in plant sources: beans, lentils, cereals
combining LBV proteins to provide all essential amino acids — e.g. beans on toast, rice and lentils
fat found mainly in animal products — associated with raised cholesterol and increased risk of heart disease
fat found mainly in plant oils and oily fish — associated with lower cholesterol. Includes monounsaturated and polyunsaturated
non-starch polysaccharide found in plant foods — aids digestion, prevents constipation and reduces risk of bowel cancer

questions.question: What are macronutrients?
options: Nutrients needed in small amounts for body functions
Nutrients needed in large amounts — carbohydrates, proteins, and fats
Vitamins and minerals found in fruit and vegetables
Substances that help build bones and teeth
questions.correct_answer: Nutrients needed in large amounts — carbohydrates, proteins, and fats
questions.explanation: Macronutrients are the three main nutrients required in large quantities by the body: carbohydrates, proteins, and fats.

questions.question: Which of the following is the main source of energy in food?
questions.explanation: Carbohydrates are the primary macronutrient used by the body for energy and are found in foods like bread, rice, pasta, and fruit.

questions.question: What is the main role of protein in the body?
options: For growth, repair, and maintenance of the body
questions.correct_answer: For growth, repair, and maintenance of the body
questions.explanation: Protein is essential for building and repairing body tissues, and it is made up of amino acids which the body uses for growth and maintenance.

questions.question: Which foods are good sources of protein?
questions.explanation: Meat, fish, eggs, and pulses (beans and lentils) are all excellent sources of protein needed for body growth and repair.

questions.question: What are essential amino acids?
options: Amino acids that are not important for health
Amino acids the body cannot make and must obtain from food
Amino acids found only in plant-based foods
Amino acids that provide energy to muscles
questions.correct_answer: Amino acids the body cannot make and must obtain from food
questions.explanation: Essential amino acids are amino acids that the human body cannot produce on its own, so they must be obtained through diet from foods like meat and eggs.

questions.question: Which type of protein contains all essential amino acids?
options: HBV protein from animal sources like meat and eggs
questions.correct_answer: HBV protein from animal sources like meat and eggs
questions.explanation: HBV (High Biological Value) proteins found in animal products such as meat, fish, eggs, and dairy contain all the essential amino acids needed by the body.

questions.question: What does LBV protein stand for?
questions.explanation: LBV stands for Low Biological Value and refers to proteins lacking one or more essential amino acids, commonly found in plant sources like beans and lentils.

questions.question: What is protein complementation?
options: Eating only protein-rich foods at each meal
Combining LBV proteins to provide all essential amino acids together
questions.correct_answer: Combining LBV proteins to provide all essential amino acids together
questions.explanation: Protein complementation involves combining plant-based LBV proteins (like beans on toast or rice and lentils) so that together they provide all essential amino acids.

questions.question: Which type of fat is mainly found in animal products?
questions.explanation: Saturated fat is found mainly in animal products and is associated with raised cholesterol levels and increased risk of heart disease.

questions.question: Which foods are good sources of unsaturated fat?
options: Red meat and fatty cuts of pork
questions.explanation: Unsaturated fats are found mainly in plant oils and oily fish and are associated with lower cholesterol levels compared to saturated fats.

questions.question: Carbohydrates can be either simple sugars or complex starch.
questions.explanation: Carbohydrates are divided into two types: simple carbohydrates (sugars) and complex carbohydrates (starch) found in foods like bread and rice.

questions.question: Protein is made up of amino acids.
questions.explanation: Protein is indeed composed of amino acids, which are linked together in different combinations to form various proteins.

questions.question: The body can produce all essential amino acids on its own.
questions.explanation: The body cannot produce essential amino acids, which is why they must be obtained from food sources.

questions.question: HBV proteins are found in animal sources like meat, fish, and eggs.
questions.explanation: HBV (High Biological Value) proteins are found in animal products and contain all essential amino acids needed by the body.

questions.question: Beans and lentils are examples of HBV proteins.
questions.explanation: Beans and lentils are plant-based LBV proteins that lack one or more essential amino acids, not HBV proteins.

questions.question: Saturated fat is associated with lower cholesterol and reduced heart disease risk.
questions.explanation: Saturated fat is associated with raised cholesterol and increased risk of heart disease, while unsaturated fat is better for heart health.

questions.question: Unsaturated fats are found mainly in plant oils and oily fish.
questions.explanation: Unsaturated fats are primarily found in plant oils and oily fish like salmon and mackerel, and are associated with better cholesterol levels.

questions.question: Dietary fibre is found only in animal products.
questions.explanation: Dietary fibre (NSP) is found in plant foods such as vegetables, fruits, and wholegrain products, not in animal products.

questions.question: Dietary fibre helps prevent constipation and reduces the risk of bowel cancer.
questions.explanation: Dietary fibre (NSP) aids digestion, prevents constipation, and is associated with reduced risk of bowel cancer.

questions.question: Bread, rice, and pasta are good sources of carbohydrates.
questions.explanation: Bread, rice, and pasta are all complex carbohydrates that provide the main source of energy for the body.

questions.question: ________ are nutrients needed in large amounts — carbohydrates, proteins, and fats.
questions.explanation: The three main macronutrients are carbohydrates, proteins, and fats, which are needed in large quantities by the body.

questions.question: ________ are the main source of energy in food, found in bread, rice, pasta, fruit, and sugar.
questions.explanation: Carbohydrates are the primary macronutrient for energy and are available as simple sugars or complex starches.

questions.question: ________ are amino acids that the body cannot make and must be obtained from food.
questions.explanation: Essential amino acids cannot be synthesised by the body and must come from dietary sources like meat and eggs.

questions.question: ________ protein contains all essential amino acids and is found in animal sources like meat, fish, and eggs.
questions.explanation: HBV (High Biological Value) protein found in animal products contains all the essential amino acids the body needs.

questions.question: ________ protein lacks one or more essential amino acids and is found in plant sources like beans and lentils.
questions.explanation: LBV (Low Biological Value) protein from plant sources does not contain all essential amino acids unless combined with other proteins.

questions.question: ________ fat is found mainly in animal products and is associated with raised cholesterol and heart disease risk.
questions.explanation: Saturated fat, found in foods like butter and fatty meat, is linked to higher cholesterol and increased cardiovascular disease risk.

questions.question: ________ fat is found mainly in plant oils and oily fish and is associated with lower cholesterol.
questions.explanation: Unsaturated fat from sources like olive oil and salmon is better for heart health and associated with lower cholesterol levels.

questions.question: ________ is a non-starch polysaccharide found in plant foods that aids digestion and prevents constipation.
questions.explanation: Dietary fibre (NSP) is found in plant-based foods and is essential for healthy digestion and reducing bowel cancer risk.

questions.question: An example of protein complementation is ________ on toast, which combines LBV proteins to provide all essential amino acids.
questions.explanation: Beans on toast combines the incomplete protein of beans with the incomplete protein of bread to create a complete amino acid profile.

questions.question: ________ and rice is another example of protein complementation that provides all essential amino acids.
questions.explanation: Lentils and rice are often eaten together in many cuisines because each provides different amino acids, creating a complete protein when combined.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: Nutrients needed in large amounts — carbohydrates, proteins, and fats
Protein containing all essential amino acids, found in animal sources like meat and eggs
Protein lacking one or more essential amino acids, found in plant sources like beans and lentils
Fat found mainly in animal products, associated with raised cholesterol and heart disease
Non-starch polysaccharide found in plant foods that aids digestion and prevents constipation

cards.definition: nutrients needed in small amounts — vitamins and minerals
a water-soluble vitamin needed for healthy skin, immune function, and iron absorption — found in citrus fruit, peppers, and broccoli
a fat-soluble vitamin needed for calcium absorption and healthy bones — made by the skin in sunlight; found in oily fish and eggs
a mineral needed to make haemoglobin in red blood cells — deficiency causes anaemia. Found in red meat, spinach, and fortified cereals
a mineral needed for strong bones and teeth and muscle function — found in dairy products, leafy greens, and fortified plant milks
vitamins B and C — dissolve in water and cannot be stored in the body. Must be eaten regularly; easily lost in cooking
vitamins A, D, E, and K — stored in the liver. Can accumulate to toxic levels if consumed in excess
a UK government guide showing the proportions of different food groups needed for a healthy, balanced diet
UK government guidelines for the amounts of energy and nutrients needed by different groups of people
calculating the nutrient content of a dish or diet — used to evaluate whether it meets recommended intakes

questions.question: What are micronutrients?
options: Nutrients needed in large amounts to provide energy
Nutrients needed in small amounts — vitamins and minerals
Nutrients that are only found in plant-based foods
Nutrients that must be cooked before eating
questions.correct_answer: Nutrients needed in small amounts — vitamins and minerals
questions.explanation: Micronutrients are vitamins and minerals required in small quantities for various body functions, unlike macronutrients which are needed in larger amounts.

questions.question: Which of the following is a good source of vitamin C?
questions.explanation: Peppers are rich in vitamin C, along with citrus fruits and broccoli. The other options are sources of different nutrients.

questions.question: What is the main role of vitamin D in the body?
options: To help the immune system fight infections
To help calcium absorption and maintain healthy bones
To make red blood cells carry oxygen
questions.correct_answer: To help calcium absorption and maintain healthy bones
questions.explanation: Vitamin D is essential for calcium absorption and bone health. It is produced by the skin in sunlight and found in oily fish and eggs.

questions.question: What deficiency disease is caused by a lack of iron?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency causes anaemia because iron is needed to make haemoglobin in red blood cells. Without enough iron, the blood cannot carry oxygen effectively.

questions.question: Which food is the best source of calcium?
questions.explanation: Dairy products like milk and cheese are excellent sources of calcium. Leafy greens and fortified plant milks also contain calcium.

questions.question: What is a characteristic of water-soluble vitamins?
options: They are stored in the liver for months
They dissolve in water and cannot be stored in the body
They are found only in animal products
questions.correct_answer: They dissolve in water and cannot be stored in the body
questions.explanation: Water-soluble vitamins (B and C) dissolve in water and are not stored in the body, so they must be eaten regularly and are easily lost during cooking.

questions.question: Which vitamins are fat-soluble and can be stored in the body?
options: Vitamins A, D, E, and K
questions.correct_answer: Vitamins A, D, E, and K
questions.explanation: Fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, and K are stored in the liver and can accumulate to toxic levels if consumed in excess.

questions.question: What is the Eatwell Guide used for?
options: To list all foods that are unhealthy
To show the proportions of different food groups needed for a healthy, balanced diet
To calculate the exact calories needed by each person
questions.correct_answer: To show the proportions of different food groups needed for a healthy, balanced diet
questions.explanation: The Eatwell Guide is a UK government tool that visually shows the recommended proportions of different food groups for a balanced diet.

questions.question: What do dietary reference values (DRVs) provide?
options: A list of foods to avoid completely
Guidelines for the amounts of energy and nutrients needed by different groups of people
Information about the cost of different nutrients
Recommendations for how often to eat each food
questions.correct_answer: Guidelines for the amounts of energy and nutrients needed by different groups of people
questions.explanation: DRVs are UK government guidelines that specify the recommended amounts of energy and nutrients for different age groups and genders.

questions.question: What is nutritional analysis used for?
options: To decide which restaurant to visit
To calculate the nutrient content of a dish or diet and evaluate if it meets recommended intakes
To determine the cost of ingredients
To find recipes on the internet
questions.correct_answer: To calculate the nutrient content of a dish or diet and evaluate if it meets recommended intakes
questions.explanation: Nutritional analysis involves calculating what nutrients are in food to check whether a meal or diet meets the recommended daily amounts.

questions.question: Vitamin C is a fat-soluble vitamin that can be stored in the body for long periods.
questions.explanation: Vitamin C is a water-soluble vitamin that cannot be stored in the body and must be eaten regularly.

questions.question: The skin can produce vitamin D when exposed to sunlight.
questions.explanation: Vitamin D is a fat-soluble vitamin that the skin produces naturally when exposed to sunlight, in addition to dietary sources.

questions.question: Spinach is a good source of iron.
questions.explanation: Spinach is indeed a good source of iron, along with red meat and fortified cereals.

questions.question: Water-soluble vitamins are easily preserved during cooking and cannot be lost.
questions.explanation: Water-soluble vitamins are easily lost during cooking because they dissolve in water, so care must be taken when preparing foods containing them.

questions.question: Fat-soluble vitamins can accumulate to toxic levels if consumed in excess.
questions.explanation: Because they are stored in the liver, fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) can build up to dangerous levels if too much is consumed.

questions.question: Oily fish is a good source of vitamin D.
questions.explanation: Oily fish like salmon and mackerel are excellent dietary sources of vitamin D.

questions.question: Calcium is only found in dairy products and cannot be obtained from plant-based foods.
questions.explanation: While dairy is a major source, calcium is also found in leafy greens and fortified plant milks.

questions.question: Haemoglobin is a protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen, and iron is needed to make it.
questions.explanation: Iron is essential for the production of haemoglobin, the protein in red blood cells responsible for oxygen transport.

questions.question: The Eatwell Guide shows exact portion sizes for individual meals.
questions.explanation: The Eatwell Guide shows the proportions of food groups needed for a healthy diet, but does not provide exact portion sizes for individual meals.

questions.question: Broccoli contains vitamin C.
questions.explanation: Broccoli is a good source of vitamin C, along with citrus fruits and peppers.

questions.question: Vitamin C is important for healthy skin, immune function, and ________ absorption.
questions.explanation: Vitamin C aids in the absorption of iron, which is important for preventing anaemia and maintaining healthy blood.

questions.question: ________ are nutrients needed in small amounts, such as vitamins and minerals.
questions.explanation: Micronutrients include vitamins and minerals required by the body in small quantities for various essential functions.

questions.question: Vitamin D is a ________-soluble vitamin that can be stored in the liver.
questions.explanation: Fat-soluble vitamins like vitamin D are stored in the liver, unlike water-soluble vitamins which are not stored.

questions.question: A lack of iron in the diet can lead to a condition called ________.
questions.explanation: Anaemia is the deficiency disease caused by insufficient iron, which impairs the blood's ability to carry oxygen.

questions.question: ________ and ________ are water-soluble vitamins that cannot be stored in the body.
questions.explanation: Vitamins B and C are water-soluble and must be regularly consumed as they are not stored and are easily lost in cooking.

questions.question: The ________ is a UK government guide showing the proportions of different food groups for a healthy diet.
questions.explanation: The Eatwell Guide is a visual tool used in the UK to help people understand what a balanced diet should look like.

questions.question: ________ (DRVs) are guidelines for the amounts of energy and nutrients needed by different groups.
questions.explanation: DRVs provide specific recommendations for nutrient and energy intake tailored to different age groups and sexes.

questions.question: ________ is a mineral found in leafy greens, dairy products, and fortified plant milks.
questions.explanation: Calcium is essential for strong bones, teeth, and muscle function, and is found in various food sources.

questions.question: ________ analysis involves calculating the nutrient content of a dish to evaluate if it meets recommended intakes.
questions.explanation: Nutritional analysis is a method used to assess whether foods and meals provide adequate nutrients for health.

questions.question: Red meat, spinach, and fortified cereals are all good sources of ________.
questions.explanation: Iron is found in these foods and is essential for making haemoglobin in red blood cells to prevent anaemia.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: Nutrients needed in small amounts — vitamins and minerals
Vitamins A, D, E, and K that are stored in the liver and can accumulate to toxic levels
A mineral needed for strong bones, teeth, and muscle function
A water-soluble vitamin needed for healthy skin, immune function, and iron absorption
UK government guidelines for the amounts of energy and nutrients needed by different groups

cards.definition: the irreversible change to the structure of a protein when heated — e.g. egg white solidifying when cooked
the setting of protein when heated — e.g. eggs setting, meat firming up when cooked
starch grains absorb water and swell when heated, thickening the mixture — e.g. in sauces and custards
the combining of two liquids that normally separate — an emulsifier holds oil and water together (e.g. lecithin in egg yolk for mayonnaise)
the browning of sugar when heated to high temperatures — creates complex flavours
a browning reaction between amino acids and sugars when heated — creates flavour and colour in bread crusts, roast meat, and toast
illness caused by eating food contaminated with harmful bacteria — e.g. Salmonella, E. coli, Campylobacter
the temperature range 8°C–63°C in which bacteria multiply most rapidly — food should not be kept in this range
the transfer of bacteria from one food to another — e.g. using the same chopping board for raw meat and salad
a food safety management system identifying and controlling risks at critical points in food production

questions.question: What happens to egg white when you cook an egg?
options: It changes colour but stays soft
questions.explanation: Denaturation is the irreversible change to protein structure when heated. Egg white contains protein that denatures during cooking, causing it to set and become solid.

questions.question: Which process describes what happens when meat firms up during cooking?
questions.explanation: Coagulation is the setting of protein when heated. When meat is cooked, its proteins coagulate and the meat becomes firmer.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of gelatinisation in cooking?
options: To brown the surface of food
To create complex flavours in sugar
questions.explanation: Gelatinisation occurs when starch grains absorb water and swell when heated, which thickens the mixture. This is commonly used in sauces and custards.

questions.question: Which ingredient acts as an emulsifier in mayonnaise?
questions.explanation: Egg yolk contains lecithin, which is an emulsifier. It allows oil and water to combine in mayonnaise, which normally would separate.

questions.question: Which cooking process creates a brown colour on sugar at high temperatures?
questions.explanation: Caramelisation is the browning of sugar when heated to high temperatures. This process creates complex flavours and a golden-brown colour.

questions.question: What causes the brown colour on the crust of bread?
options: Maillard reaction between amino acids and sugars
questions.correct_answer: Maillard reaction between amino acids and sugars
questions.explanation: The Maillard reaction is a browning reaction between amino acids and sugars when heated. This creates the flavour and colour in bread crusts, roast meat, and toast.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of food poisoning bacteria?
questions.explanation: Salmonella is a harmful bacterium that causes food poisoning. Other examples include E. coli and Campylobacter, which contaminate food and make people ill.

questions.question: At what temperature range do bacteria multiply most rapidly?
questions.explanation: The danger zone is the temperature range 8°C–63°C where bacteria multiply most rapidly. Food should not be kept in this range to prevent food poisoning.

questions.question: What is cross-contamination in food preparation?
options: Using too much salt in food
The transfer of bacteria from one food to another
questions.correct_answer: The transfer of bacteria from one food to another
questions.explanation: Cross-contamination is the transfer of bacteria from one food to another, such as using the same chopping board for raw meat and salad without cleaning it first.

questions.question: What does HACCP stand for?
questions.explanation: HACCP is a food safety management system that identifies and controls risks at critical points in food production. It helps prevent food poisoning and contamination.

questions.question: Denaturation of protein is reversible when the food cools down.
questions.explanation: Denaturation is an irreversible change to protein structure. Once an egg white is cooked and solidified, it cannot return to its original liquid state.

questions.question: Starch grains swell and absorb water during gelatinisation.
questions.explanation: During gelatinisation, starch grains absorb water and swell when heated, which thickens the mixture. This is a key process in making sauces and custards.

questions.question: An emulsifier can help oil and water mix together permanently.
questions.explanation: An emulsifier like lecithin in egg yolk allows oil and water to combine and stay mixed together, which is essential for making mayonnaise and other emulsified sauces.

questions.question: Caramelisation and the Maillard reaction are the same cooking process.
questions.explanation: These are different processes. Caramelisation is the browning of sugar alone, while the Maillard reaction is a browning reaction between amino acids and sugars.

questions.question: E. coli is a bacterium that can cause food poisoning.
questions.explanation: E. coli is a harmful bacterium that causes food poisoning. It can contaminate food through poor hygiene practices and cross-contamination.

questions.question: Food kept at 5°C is in the danger zone for bacterial growth.
questions.explanation: The danger zone is 8°C–63°C. At 5°C, food is below this range and bacteria multiply more slowly, making it safer for storage in a fridge.

questions.question: Using the same chopping board for raw chicken and vegetables can cause cross-contamination.
questions.explanation: Cross-contamination occurs when bacteria from raw meat transfer to other foods. Using separate chopping boards or thoroughly cleaning between uses prevents this.

questions.question: HACCP is only used in restaurants and not in home kitchens.
questions.explanation: While HACCP is formally used in commercial food production, its principles of identifying and controlling food safety risks can and should be applied in home kitchens too.

questions.question: Cooking meat to a high temperature is a critical control point in HACCP.
questions.explanation: Cooking meat to the correct temperature is a critical control point because it kills harmful bacteria and prevents food poisoning.

questions.question: The Maillard reaction only occurs on the surface of roasted meat.
questions.explanation: While the Maillard reaction is most visible on the surface of roasted meat where browning occurs, it can happen throughout the food during cooking at high temperatures.

questions.question: When egg white is cooked, the protein undergoes ________ and becomes solid.
questions.explanation: Denaturation is the irreversible change to protein structure when heated, causing the egg white to set and solidify.

questions.question: The process where starch grains swell and thicken a sauce is called ________.
questions.explanation: Gelatinisation is when starch grains absorb water and swell when heated, thickening mixtures like sauces and custards.

questions.question: An ________ is a substance that helps combine oil and water, such as lecithin in egg yolk.
questions.explanation: An emulsifier holds oil and water together, allowing them to mix and stay combined, which is essential for making mayonnaise.

questions.question: The browning reaction between amino acids and sugars during cooking is called the ________ reaction.
questions.explanation: The Maillard reaction is a browning reaction between amino acids and sugars that creates flavour and colour in bread crusts, roast meat, and toast.

questions.question: Illness caused by eating food contaminated with harmful bacteria like Salmonella is called ________.
questions.explanation: Food poisoning is an illness caused by eating food contaminated with harmful bacteria such as Salmonella, E. coli, or Campylobacter.

questions.question: The temperature range 8°C–63°C is known as the ________ because bacteria multiply most rapidly in this range.
questions.explanation: The danger zone is the temperature range where bacteria multiply most rapidly, so food should not be kept in this range to prevent food poisoning.

questions.question: Using the same unwashed chopping board for raw meat and salad can cause ________ of harmful bacteria.
questions.explanation: Cross-contamination is the transfer of bacteria from one food to another, which can be prevented by using separate chopping boards or cleaning thoroughly.

questions.question: ________ is a food safety system that identifies and controls risks at critical points in food production.
questions.explanation: HACCP (Hazard Analysis Critical Control Points) is a management system used to prevent food poisoning by controlling risks throughout food production.

questions.question: The browning of sugar at high temperatures, which creates complex flavours, is called ________.
questions.explanation: Caramelisation is the browning of sugar when heated to high temperatures, creating complex flavours and a golden-brown colour.

questions.question: The setting of protein when heated, such as eggs setting or meat firming up, is called ________.
questions.explanation: Coagulation is the setting of protein when heated, which causes changes in texture such as eggs setting or meat becoming firmer during cooking.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: The irreversible change to protein structure when heated
The combining of two liquids that normally separate using an emulsifier
The temperature range 8°C–63°C where bacteria multiply most rapidly
A browning reaction between amino acids and sugars that creates flavour and colour
A food safety management system for identifying and controlling risks
